disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Sudden behavioural escalation over ~4 hours beginning shortly after an environmental change (a new, unfamiliar staff member started the shift)
trigger_question_examples: What was happening just before this started?
Has anything changed in his routine or environment today?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: It started about four hours ago, not long after a new staff member came on for the evening shift. He doesn't cope well with unfamiliar people and it just built up from there.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Active self-injurious behaviour — repeatedly striking both wrists against hard surfaces, now causing redness and swelling
trigger_question_examples: What is he doing to his wrists?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: He keeps hitting both wrists hard against the wall and the furniture while flapping his hands. They're going red and a bit swollen and we're worried he'll really injure them.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Panic and hyperventilation with signs of high autonomic arousal (fast breathing, racing heart, flushed, very distressed)
trigger_question_examples: Does he seem panicked or is his heart racing?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: He's hyperventilating and looks like his heart is pounding — really flushed and panicky, like he can't come down from it.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: The escalation has not responded to the usual de-escalation strategies over several hours
trigger_question_examples: Have you tried the things that usually calm him?
Is anything working to settle him?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: We've tried everything that normally works — quiet space, his routine — and none of it is settling him tonight. That's why we're worried.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Patient has a lifelong genetic condition and intellectual disability and cannot say what is wrong himself — all history is via the support worker
trigger_question_examples: Can he tell you what's bothering him?
Does he have any conditions we should know about?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: He has Fragile X and an intellectual disability, so he can't really tell us what's wrong — especially when he's like this. We're going off what we can see.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: On targeted screening for a physical trigger: no fever reported, he ate and drank normally today, no recent illness, and no obvious tooth, ear or tummy pain noticed
trigger_question_examples: Could something be hurting him — a tooth, an ear, his tummy, constipation?
Any fever, recent illness, or change in eating, drinking or bowels?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Now that you ask — no fever that we've noticed, he ate and drank normally today and hasn't been unwell. We haven't seen him hold his face or tummy, but honestly he can't tell us if something hurts.
disclosure_items.patient_deflection_template: We just assumed it was the change in staff setting him off.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: On targeted screening: he stays awake and responds (avoidantly) to voice, the hand movements are purposeful flapping rather than rhythmic jerking, and there is no loss of awareness or incontinence
trigger_question_examples: Is he awake and responding to you?
Are the movements something he's doing on purpose, or are they jerky and out of his control?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: He's awake and does respond to us, just won't look at us — the flapping is his usual thing, not jerky shaking, and he hasn't blanked out or wet himself.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: On targeted screening of the wrists: he can still move and flap both wrists forcefully, and when he pauses he doesn't seem to be guarding one specific spot
trigger_question_examples: Can he still move both wrists, or is he protecting one?
When he stops, does one spot look obviously worse or bent?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: He's using both hands forcefully to flap, so nothing seems obviously broken, but they're red and swollen and we can't really examine him while he's like this.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: A PRN calming medication is kept for him but he has refused it during this episode
trigger_question_examples: Does he have any medication for episodes like this?
Has he taken anything to calm down?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: He's got a PRN clonidine tablet for times like this, but he's too panicked to take it and won't swallow it.
